features.name: Spot & Preemptible VMs
features.description: Highly discounted (up to 91%) compute instances that Google can reclaim with a 30-second warning; Spot has no max duration while Preemptible is capped at 24 hours.

features.name: Live Migration
features.description: GCP technology that automatically migrates running VM instances to another host in the same zone during host maintenance, ensuring zero downtime.

features.name: Sole-Tenant Nodes
features.description: Dedicated physical compute servers running only your VMs, crucial for security, compliance, and custom hardware/licensing requirements.

features.name: Committed Use Discounts (CUDs)
features.description: Deep discounts (resource-based or flexible vCPU/RAM commitments) in exchange for signing a 1-year or 3-year contract.

features.name: Persistent Disk Snapshots
features.description: Zonal or regional block storage snapshots stored in Cloud Storage, supporting incremental backups and scheduling.

iamRoles.description: Full control over all Compute Engine resources, network, and security.
Permission to create, modify, and delete virtual machine instances, disks, and images.
Permissions to configure networking, firewalls, and routing, but not VM creation.

cliExamples.description: Create a custom-sized VM with startup script and cloud-platform scope
SSH into an instance through Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) without public IP

design: Compute Engine virtual machines run on KVM virtualization and support custom vCPU and memory configurations.
Zonal persistent disks are replicated across multiple physical disks in a single zone to prevent data loss.
Regional persistent disks replicate data synchronously across two zones in the same region, providing an RPO of zero.
Local SSDs are physically attached to the server hosting the VM, offering extremely high IOPS and low latency.
Data on Local SSDs is ephemeral and is lost when the VM instance is stopped, deleted, or undergoes host failure.
Sole-tenant nodes allow you to run instances on dedicated physical hardware for compliance and software licensing.
Custom metadata can be queried by the VM from the metadata server at http://169.254.169.254/computeMetadata/v1/.
Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) support autoscaling, autohealing, and multi-zone deployment of identical VM templates.
security: Access scopes set the default authorization scopes for API requests made from the VM's service account.
Best practice is to assign the 'cloud-platform' scope to the VM and restrict permissions using IAM roles on the service account.
OS Login allows managing SSH access to VM instances using IAM credentials, replacing metadata-based SSH keys.
Service Account User role (roles/iam.serviceAccountUser) is required to attach a service account to a VM.
Shielded VMs protect instances from boot-level malware and rootkits by validating boot state using UEFI and vTPM.
Firewall rules are applied at the network level but evaluated at the individual VM instance level using network tags.
Compute instances without external IP addresses can access Google APIs using Private Google Access in the subnet.
Disk encryption defaults to Google-managed keys, but can be configured with CMEK or Customer-Supplied Keys (CSEK).
reliability: Live Migration is enabled by default, allowing VMs to move automatically to other hardware during infrastructure updates.
VM shutdown scripts are guaranteed to run on a best-effort basis for up to 90 seconds (longer on request).
Regional MIGs distribute VM instances across multiple zones in a region, protecting against single-zone outages.
Autohealing uses HTTP(S) health checks to automatically recreate unhealthy VM instances in a Managed Instance Group.
Backup VM configurations using custom images or machine images, which capture disk state, metadata, and configuration.
Spot VMs have an SLA of 0% and are subject to termination at any time, requiring stateless, fault-tolerant workloads.
Always design instances to be stateless; configure startup scripts to pull configuration from Cloud Storage on boot.
Zonal outages do not affect regional persistent disks, allowing prompt recovery by attaching disks to a VM in another zone.
cost: Resource-based Committed Use Discounts (CUDs) apply to vCPUs and memory for predictable workloads over a 1 or 3-year term.
Flexible CUDs provide spending-based discounts across VM families and regions, offering greater architectural flexibility.
Spot VMs offer a 60% to 91% discount compared to on-demand pricing, ideal for batch jobs and testing.
Custom machine types allow matching resources exactly to application needs, avoiding cost overhead from over-provisioning.
Persistent disks are billed based on provisioned capacity, not used capacity, so size them carefully.
Delete unattached persistent disks and unused static external IP addresses to avoid idle resource charges.
Enable autoscaling in MIGs to scale down instance counts during low-demand periods and save compute costs.
Configure VM scheduling (using Cloud Scheduler and Cloud Functions) to shut down dev/test VMs overnight.
